notes: kiro-cli `!command` shell-escape (the TUI's local-shell escape, the direct analog of Claude Code's !cmd — surface confirmed by kiro's changelog line 'Reject queued ! shell-escape commands during processing'). kiro's Source is FilesUnderRoot{Dir:.kiro/sessions/cli, JSONLineParser}: the daemon tails the <uuid>.jsonl. The kirocli parser emits state ONLY for Prompt/AssistantMessage/ToolResults/Clear; every other kind hits `default: ev.Skip` (parser.go:110-112) and there is NO shell-escape handler. A `!echo hello` escape runs LOCALLY with no LLM round-trip, so it produces no AssistantMessage (no working, no turn_done). What it writes to the .jsonl is UNKNOWN for kiro (the live 2.5.1 smoke saw only Prompt/AssistantMessage/ToolResults/Clear, .build/refresh/kiro-cli-smoke/FINDINGS.md §4) — but BOTH readings satisfy the invariant: a no-trace escape holds it trivially, and an unrecognized kind hits default:Skip. So the tailer fires no event and the session stays ready across the escape (same shape as pi's skipped bashExecution and the no-trace half of aider). A warm-up 'warm' turn drives ready->working->ready to materialize the <uuid>.jsonl so resolve_transcript can bind. After warm_turn_end returns to ready, escape_window pins the session ready with duration_at_least_ms:4000 and invariants forbidding state_transition to working/waiting — those are exactly what would (wrongly) fire if the `!` escape drove a model call or were mapped to a transition; the LOAD-BEARING assertion is that the user-side shell escape does NOT flap the session's state. The trailing 'alive' turn drives a SECOND ready->working->ready cycle proving the session is still alive after the escape (the scenario's liveness arc; state topology ready->working->ready, final state ready, tool_calls <= 0). RECORDABLE via the TUI-only driver: drive-kiro-cli-interactive.sh elicits send/slash/wait_turn/sleep (DRIVE_ELICITS) and delivers the `!echo hello` escape as plain text straight to the live kiro-cli TUI via tmux send-keys -l (the leading ! is sent verbatim; NOT a slash, NOT an arrow-key picker, so no keys gap; NOT headless --no-interactive, which writes no session file). No wait_turn follows the escape (it writes no text-only AssistantMessage, so turn_count never increments and wait_turn would time out); a 6s sleep holds the ready dwell open instead. EMISSION CAVEAT: this spec is doc+source derived; whether kiro writes nothing vs an unrecognized-but-skipped line for the escape is provisional until record confirms it (the invariant holds either way). The kiro JSONL carries no model/token/cost (those live only in the <uuid>.json sidecar the v1 parser does not read, FINDINGS.md §4), so observations assert model:unknown, cost_nonzero:false, tokens_nonzero:false. HOW RUNS DIFFER: warm-up/alive assistant wording, session UUID, PID, and sub-second timing vary; stable structurally: a warm-up arc on one <uuid> session, a continuous ready dwell (>=4s) across the escape window with NO working/waiting transition, a liveness arc on the SAME session, a single process_exited, and a final ready state.

text: kiro-cli session (the minted <uuid>) appears in ready within 2s of TUI launch
Warm-up 'warm' prompt drives ready -> working (real LLM turn; materializes the <uuid>.jsonl so subsequent steps can resolve_transcript and bind the session)
Warm-up turn completes back to ready (same <uuid> session id); state never entered waiting
PID bound to the <uuid> kiro-cli session

The `!echo hello` shell escape runs locally with no LLM round-trip — the session stays ready throughout. NO state_transition to working is recorded for the escape (it never reaches the four kinds the kirocli parser maps — it writes nothing, or an unrecognized kind that hits default:Skip), and state never entered waiting. The escape does NOT flap the session's state and is NOT left stuck in working. This is the load-bearing user-observable signal of the scenario; the escape is observed only as this ready dwell, never as a distinct shell-escape event
Trailing 'alive' prompt drives ready -> working a SECOND time on the same <uuid> session — proves the session is still alive after the escape window (scenario liveness arc)
Trailing turn completes back to ready — final state ready (state topology ready->working->ready); state never entered waiting
REPL torn down once after the liveness turn -> a single process_exited on the <uuid> session; state immediately before teardown is ready
